title: Quadratic Sums-of-Powers for Fixed-Parameter Tractable Quantum-Circuit Simulation
authors: Alexis de Colnet, Floris Geerts, Rihan Hai, Alfons Laarman, Joon Hyung Lee, Guillermo A. Pérez
abstract: Strongly simulating a quantum circuit, that is, computing an output amplitude, amounts to summing the circuit's Feynman paths, a weighted count over assignments to the Boolean ``path'' variables. The circuit's gates induce correlations among these variables, forming a graph whose structure determines the hardness of the simulation task. This sum-of-powers viewpoint underlies recent simulators built on knowledge-representation tools from artificial intelligence, namely binary decision diagrams and weighted model counting. We show that the structural quantity most accurately governing the difficulty is the rank-width of the path-variable graph, and we give an algorithm that evaluates the amplitude in time that is exponential only in this rank-width and polynomial in the circuit size. Rank-width can be far smaller than the widths that control competing methods: as corollaries, our algorithm reproduces a recent decision-diagram simulation breakthrough as a special case and matches the Markov--Shi tensor-network contraction bound. To complement this, we exhibit circuit families on which our algorithm provably beats both competing methods. The new method applies to every circuit built from Hadamard and diagonal gates, in particular to circuits over Clifford+T. In practical terms, general-purpose decision-diagram and model-counting tools can serve as the workhorse, with our specialized algorithm dispatched to exploit a small rank-width of the associated graph when it is present.
summary: This paper develops a novel, highly efficient algorithm to simulate quantum circuits by reformulating the amplitude calculation as evaluating a quadratic Sum-of-Powers (SOP) polynomial. The complexity is shown to depend only on the rank-width of the associated variable graph, which is proven to be a superior structural measure compared to treewidth or linear rank-width for this problem.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of efficiently computing the output amplitude of a quantum circuit, which is mathematically equivalent to evaluating a quadratic Sum-of-Powers (SOP) polynomial over binary variables.
main_result: They introduce an algorithm whose time complexity is exponential only in the rank-width of the associated path-variable graph, providing a provably superior simulation bound compared to methods based on treewidth or tensor network contraction complexity.
method: The core method is a dynamic programming approach parameterized by the rank-width of the SOP variable graph, which is optimized by transforming the residue counting problem into the Fourier domain to remove the modulus factor from the exponent.
model_or_system: The system is general quantum circuits, specifically those built from Hadamard and diagonal gates, and applicable to the universal Clifford+T gate set. The simulation is framed as calculating the amplitude $\langle z|C|yangle$ by mapping it to a quadratic SOP form.
key_observables: The output amplitude $\langle z|C|yangle$, which is determined by the count $N_j$ of assignments $\mathbf{x}$ such that the quadratic SOP polynomial $f(\mathbf{x})$ equals $j \pmod{r}$.
important_parameters: The rank-width ($	ext{rw}$) of the SOP variable graph $G_C$ is the critical structural parameter governing the complexity. The modulus $r$ (e.g., $r=8$ for $\{H, T, CZ\}$) is also crucial.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the circuit can be successfully mapped to a quadratic SOP form, and the complexity improvement relies on the rank-width being significantly smaller than the treewidth or linear rank-width.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the relationship between the SOP variable graph $G_C$ and the tensor-network line graph $L(NC)$, and demonstrate the separation between rank-width, linear rank-width, and treewidth using specific graph constructions.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the SOP viewpoint for quantum simulation, compares this to existing methods (tensor networks, BDDs), identifies rank-width as the key structural parameter, and then develops and analyzes a specialized dynamic programming algorithm parameterized by this rank-width.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, structure-aware tool for quantum simulation that moves beyond general graph parameters like treewidth, offering a potentially much tighter complexity bound for specific quantum circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
